steps.title: From Passive Tool to Autonomous Agent
steps.body:
So far, you've been the one driving — you prompt, AI responds, you act. Today that changes.

AI Agents are systems that make decisions and take actions on their own, within boundaries you set.

An agent has three things:
• A goal — what it's trying to accomplish
• Tools — what it can use (APIs, databases, search, email)
• Autonomy — permission to decide HOW to achieve the goal

The operator's job shifts from 'doing the work' to 'defining the mission and boundaries.'

An AI without tools is a thinker. An AI with tools is a worker.

Agents become powerful when they can:
→ Search the web for current information
→ Query a database for customer records
→ Send an email or Slack message
→ Update a spreadsheet or CRM
→ Trigger other automations

Each tool is a capability. The agent decides when to use which tool to achieve its goal.

steps.question: What distinguishes an AI Agent from a standard chatbot?
options: An agent uses a better AI model
An agent can take autonomous actions using tools to achieve a goal
An agent has a more friendly personality
feedback: Exactly. An agent doesn't just respond — it acts. It uses tools, makes decisions, and executes multi-step plans to accomplish its assigned goal.
Not quite. The key difference is agency — the ability to take independent actions using available tools. A chatbot responds; an agent operates.

steps.title: Meet Alex's AI Agent Team
steps.body:
Alex runs a 6-person consulting firm. Instead of hiring 3 more people, he built AI agents:

Agent 1 — Lead Researcher: Scans industry news, identifies potential clients, enriches LinkedIn profiles, outputs a prioritized leads list every Monday morning

Agent 2 — Proposal Writer: Takes lead data from Agent 1, creates personalized outreach proposals using the firm's template library, drafts in the firm's voice

Agent 3 — Follow-up Coordinator: Monitors sent proposals for 7-day no-response, sends personalized follow-ups, flags hot leads to Alex

Alex's role: Review and approve. The agents do everything else.

Result: 3x more proposals sent, 40% higher close rate, Alex works 30 hours/week instead of 55.

steps.question: AI agents are best used for fully autonomous operations where humans never need to review outputs.
feedback: Correct. The most effective pattern is human-in-the-loop — agents handle the heavy lifting, humans review critical decisions. Full autonomy is dangerous for anything with real consequences.
Not quite. The current best practice is supervised autonomy — agents execute within guardrails, humans review at decision points. Full autonomy is reserved for low-risk, high-volume tasks.

steps.title: Agent Architecture: The Three Tiers
steps.prompt:
Tier 1 — Simple Agent: One goal, one tool. Example: 'Monitor this RSS feed, email me when a new article matches these keywords.'

Tier 2 — Composite Agent: One goal, multiple tools. Example: 'Research the top 5 competitors in this space, write a comparison report, and save it to our shared drive.'

Tier 3 — Multi-Agent System: Multiple agents collaborating. Example: Researcher agent → Writer agent → Reviewer agent → Publisher agent — each with specific tools and handoff criteria.

Most operators start at Tier 1 and graduate to Tier 2 within weeks. Tier 3 is the destination.

steps.body:
The agency spectrum:

Manual (you do everything) → Automated (AI does fixed sequences) → Agentic (AI decides sequences) → Autonomous (AI operates independently)

AI operators live between 'Agentic' and 'Autonomous' with guardrails. The key insight: autonomy is a dial, not a switch. You turn it up gradually as reliability improves.
highlights: autonomy is a dial, not a switch

steps.question: A task that requires: (1) searching multiple data sources, (2) comparing results against known patterns, (3) making judgment calls based on partial information, and (4) taking action based on those judgments.
feedback: This is a prime candidate for an AI agent. The multi-source search, pattern comparison, and autonomous decision-making are exactly what agents excel at. Define the boundaries and let the agent work.
This task has all the hallmarks of an agent-worthy problem: multiple data sources, judgment calls, and autonomous action. Set clear guardrails and let the agent handle the exploration.

steps.title: Design Your First AI Agent
steps.instruction:
Design an AI agent that would save you real time in your work. Be specific.

Include in your design:
• Agent Name and Goal — What's its primary mission?
• Tools It Needs — What can it access? (search, database, email, Slack, CRM, etc.)
• Autonomy Level — Fully autonomous or human-in-the-loop? At what decision points?
• Inputs — What triggers it to start working?
• Outputs — What does it produce and where does it deliver results?
• Guardrails — What are the hard boundaries it shouldn't cross?
steps.placeholder:
Agent: Support Triage Agent
Goal: Categorize and route incoming support tickets to the right team member
Tools: Email inbox, knowledge base, team calendar, Slack
Autonomy: Categorize and route autonomously. Escalate to human for: angry customers, refund requests, anything outside playbook
Inputs: New email to support@
Outputs: Labeled ticket in our CRM, Slack notification to assigned team member
Guardrails: Never promise refunds without manager approval. Never share internal documentation with customers. Always include ticket ID in communications.

steps.question: What is the biggest risk when deploying AI agents with too much autonomy?
options: They might make incorrect decisions at scale before anyone notices
They might cost too much in API fees
They might be too helpful and do too much work
feedback: Correct. The compounding risk is the biggest danger — an incorrect decision made 1000 times before anyone catches it. This is why human-in-the-loop review at critical decision points is essential.
Not quite. While cost and speed are considerations, the primary risk is undetected errors compounding at scale. Start with higher human oversight and reduce it as the agent proves reliable.

steps.title: Building an Agent with No-Code Tools
steps.prompt:
You don't need to code to build agents. Here's a real example built in 2 hours:

Platform: Relevance AI (no-code agent builder)

Agent: Social Media Content Curator

Goal: 'Find 5 relevant industry articles per day, summarize each in 2 sentences, and post to our company Slack #content channel'

Tools:
• Web search (Bing API) — to find articles
• Web fetch — to read article content
• Slack API — to post messages

Configuration:
Instructions: 'Search for articles related to [topic]. Select the 5 most relevant published in the last 24 hours. Read each one. Write a 2-sentence summary. Post to Slack with source link.'
Schedule: Runs every weekday at 8 AM

Output to Slack:
📰 Tuesday's Industry Digest
1. [Title] — Summary... [link]
2. [Title] — Summary... [link]

Total setup time: 2 hours. Daily time saved: 30 minutes of manual curation.

questions.prompt: If you could have an AI agent handle one entire area of your work for a week, what would it be and why? What's the biggest concern you'd have about letting it run unsupervised?
questions.placeholder: The area, why it matters, what concerns you about autonomy...

questions.prompt: What's the smallest, lowest-risk task you could hand off to an AI agent tomorrow? Define the goal, the tool it needs, and the boundary.
questions.placeholder: The small agent, its tools, and its guardrails...

steps.title: Day 4 Complete!
steps.body:
You now understand AI agents — what they are, how they work, and how to design them. You've gone from using AI as a tool to deploying AI as a worker.

The key takeaways:
• Agents = Goal + Tools + Autonomy
• Human-in-the-loop is the safe starting point
• No-code tools let you build agents today

Tomorrow: Advanced prompt engineering. You'll learn how to make AI think better — not just respond faster.
